—The powerful and respected people of the world have a special responsibility to honor and worship him; but, unfortunately, few try to worship him with true reverence. When we come before him as the Redeemer of sinners, through repentance, faith, and love, he will accept our imperfect efforts, forgive the sin that is mixed in with them, and approve of the degree of holiness that the Holy Spirit enables us to practice. This describes the nature of religious worship: it is giving to the Lord the glory due to his name. We must be holy in all our religious services, devoted to God, and to his will and glory. There is a beauty in holiness, and that adds beauty to every act of worship. The psalmist here shows God’s authority over the natural world. In thunder, lightning, and storms, we can see and hear his glory. Let our hearts be filled with great, high, and honorable thoughts of God, whose holy adoration is so essential to true godliness. O Lord our God, you are very great! The power of the lightning matches the terror of the thunder. The fear caused by these displays of divine power should remind us of God’s mighty power, humanity's weakness, and the vulnerable and desperate condition of the wicked on judgment day. But the effects of the Divine word upon people’s souls, under the influence of the Holy Spirit, are far greater than those of thunderstorms in the natural world. Through this, even the strongest are made to tremble, the proudest are brought down, the secrets of the heart are revealed, sinners are converted, and those who are savage, sensual, and unclean become harmless, gentle, and pure. If we have heard God’s voice and have fled for refuge to the hope that is before us, let us remember that children need not fear their Father's voice when he speaks in anger to his enemies. While those without shelter tremble, let those who abide in his appointed refuge bless him for their security, looking forward to judgment day without dread, safe as Noah in the ark.
